senses.definition: Different ways or degrees in which something shows the property of being additive, often in science or mathematics.
examples: The researcher compared the additivities of the two chemical reactions in the study.
In this model, the different additivities of the variables must be taken into account.

senses.definition: The property of something in which the total effect is the sum of the effects of its parts, often used about functions, measures, or physical quantities.
examples: The additivity of the function means that f(x + y) equals f(x) plus f(y).
In this system, energy is assumed to have additivity across components.

senses.definition: The idea that the total effect of several influences equals the sum of their separate effects, especially in chemistry or pharmacology.
examples: Drug interactions are often modeled under an assumption of simple additivity.
The color intensity followed additivity when the dyes were mixed.

senses.definition: Serving to add or increase something; having the nature of an addition.
examples: The contract contained several additory clauses that raised the overall cost.
These additory factors make the analysis more complex.

senses.definition: Something that is added; an addition, especially in a technical or formal context.
examples: Each additum to the original agreement required both parties’ signatures.
The second chapter can be read as an additum to the main argument.

senses.definition: A legal procedure in which a judge increases the amount of money in a jury’s award of damages.
examples: The plaintiff requested an additur, arguing that the jury’s award was too low.
The court granted an additur rather than ordering a new trial.

senses.definition: To make someone confused and unable to think clearly.
examples: All those complicated instructions just addled my brain.
The heat seemed to addle his thinking and slow his reactions.

senses.definition: Of an egg, to become rotten and no longer good.
examples: The unrefrigerated eggs had addled in the summer heat.
If they are not collected in time, the eggs may addle before hatching.

senses.definition: Confused and not thinking clearly.
examples: After working all night, she felt completely addle and exhausted.
He gave me an addle look, as if he had no idea what I meant.

senses.definition: A person who is very silly or easily confused.
examples: Don’t be such an addlebrain; read the directions first.
He called himself an addlebrain for forgetting the keys again.

senses.definition: Stupid or easily confused; not thinking clearly.
examples: That was an addlebrained decision that cost the company a lot of money.
He can be a bit addlebrained before his morning coffee.

senses.definition: Unable to think clearly; confused.
examples: After the accident, he seemed shaken and addled but otherwise unhurt.
My brain feels too addled to study right now.

senses.definition: Rotten and no longer usable, especially referring to eggs.
examples: We had to throw away the addled eggs from the back of the cupboard.
The smell of addled eggs filled the room.

senses.definition: A silly or easily confused person.
examples: You addlehead, you left the door unlocked again.
He joked that only an addlehead would make that mistake twice.

senses.definition: Silly and easily confused; not thinking carefully.
examples: It was an addleheaded move to sign the contract without reading it.
Her addleheaded behavior annoyed her more serious colleagues.

senses.definition: In a silly or confused way, without clear thinking.
examples: He addleheadedly agreed to the deal without checking the details.
She addleheadedly left her passport on the train.

senses.definition: The quality of being silly, confused, or not thinking clearly.
examples: His addleheadedness often got him into trouble at work.
They laughed about the sheer addleheadedness of the plan.

senses.definition: A state of mental confusion or muddle.
examples: The shock left him in a state of complete addlement.
There was a general addlement in the office after the sudden announcement.

senses.definition: Things that cause confusion or mental disturbance.
examples: He spoke of the addlements of city life that tired his spirit.
All these addlements keep me from focusing on my work.

senses.definition: The condition of being confused or muddled in mind.
examples: She blamed her addleness on lack of sleep.
His speech showed a surprising addleness for such an experienced leader.

senses.definition: A person who is stupid or easily confused.
examples: Only an addlepate would sign that contract without reading it.
He felt like an addlepate after locking himself out three times.

senses.definition: Confused or not able to think clearly; often also silly or eccentric.
examples: I felt a bit addlepated after the long flight and hardly slept at all.
The addlepated old inventor talked to his machines as if they were alive.

senses.definition: The state of being confused, silly, or not thinking clearly.
examples: Her addlepatedness was obvious as she mixed up all the names.
They laughed at the sheer addlepatedness of the whole scheme.

senses.definition: A foolish or badly thought-out plan.
examples: Running away without money was an obvious addleplot.
The whole project turned out to be an addleplot from the start.

senses.definition: Third person singular of addle: makes someone confused or unable to think clearly.
examples: Too much information at once addles the listener.
The pressure addles his judgment before big matches.

senses.definition: Third person singular of addle: of an egg, becomes rotten and no longer usable.
examples: If left in the sun, the egg soon addles.
Food quickly addles in such heat.

senses.definition: Present participle of addle: making someone confused or unable to think clearly.
examples: The constant noise was addling his concentration.
Stress is addling her ability to make decisions.

senses.definition: Present participle of addle: of eggs, becoming rotten and unusable.
examples: The farmer threw away the addling eggs from the nest.
In the heat, the uncollected eggs were addling fast.

senses.definition: Rotten eggs that will not hatch; spoiled eggs.
examples: The nest was full of addlings that had to be cleared out.
They sorted the fresh eggs from the addlings.

senses.definition: A dialect or nonstandard form meaning addlings, rotten or spoiled eggs.
examples: The farmer complained that half the clutch were addlins.
They threw the addlins to the pigs.

senses.definition: Short for addition, especially in notes or technical writing.
examples: See the latest addn on page 3 of the report.
Addn and corrections will be sent in the next email.

senses.definition: Short for additional.
examples: Addnl information is provided in Appendix A.
There may be addnl costs not listed here.

senses.definition: To judge or decide something, especially in a formal or legal way; now obsolete.
examples: The council did addoom the dispute between the neighbours.
He was addoomed guilty by the ancient court.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of addoom: judged or decided, especially in a formal or legal way; now obsolete.
examples: He was addoomed guilty and led away in chains.
The land was addoomed to belong to the crown.

senses.definition: Present participle of addoom: judging or deciding something formally; now obsolete.
examples: The elders sat in the hall, addooming disputes brought before them.
He spent the day addooming cases of land and title.

senses.definition: Third person singular of addoom: judges or decides something formally; now obsolete.
examples: The council addooms each case according to ancient custom.
Who addooms which side is right in this quarrel?

senses.definition: Shown with backs turned to each other, especially referring to animals in coats of arms.
examples: The shield displayed two lions addorsed in gold.
The crest bears eagles addorsed above the helmet.

senses.definition: Placed back to back or leaning against each other; used in architecture and heraldry.
examples: The addossed columns formed a strong central support.
In the coat of arms, two keys are shown addossed.

senses.definition: Short for address, especially in technical or informal written contexts.
examples: Please send your shipping addr with the payment.
The IP addr is listed at the top of the log file.

senses.definition: The details of where someone lives or where an organization is located.
examples: What is your home address?
Please write your name and address at the top of the form.

senses.definition: A formal speech given to an audience.
examples: The president gave an address to the nation.
She delivered a moving address at the graduation ceremony.

senses.definition: To write the name and details of where someone lives on a letter or package.
examples: Did you address the envelope correctly?
The parcel was addressed to the wrong person.

senses.definition: To speak to someone directly, or to speak to a group of people in a formal way.
examples: She addressed the audience with confidence.
Please address your questions to the speaker at the end.

senses.definition: To deal with a problem or issue; to try to find a solution.
examples: We need to address the problem of late payments.
The report addresses several key questions about climate change.

senses.definition: Different ways or degrees in which systems or devices can be individually identified and reached.
examples: The engineer compared the addressabilities of the two network designs.
New standards aim to improve the addressabilities of smart devices.

senses.definition: The ability of a system or device to be individually identified and reached among many similar units.
examples: Modern cable systems use addressability to send different content to different homes.
Better addressability makes it easier to manage large networks of devices.

senses.definition: In computing, the way a computer identifies and accesses different memory locations.
examples: The system’s addressability is limited by the width of its address bus.
Increasing addressability allows the computer to access more memory.

senses.definition: Possible to deal with or solve.
examples: These risks are serious but addressable with proper planning.
Most of the problems you mentioned are addressable in the short term.

senses.definition: In technology, able to be individually identified and reached by a control system.
examples: Each sensor in the network is addressable by its unique ID.
Addressable devices allow targeted software updates.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of address: wrote the name and details of where someone lives on something.
examples: She addressed the letter to her grandmother.
The package was addressed incorrectly and returned.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of address: spoke to a person or group, especially in a formal way.
examples: The mayor addressed the crowd after the ceremony.
She addressed her comments to the committee chair.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of address: dealt with a problem or issue.
examples: The report addressed several important concerns.
We addressed the safety issues before reopening the building.

senses.definition: The person whose name is written on a letter, package, or email; the person who is meant to receive it.
examples: If the addressee has moved, the letter will be returned.
When emailing, make sure the addressee’s details are correct.

senses.definition: Plural of addressee: people who are meant to receive a message, letter, or email.
examples: Please check that all addressees are listed in the email.
The letter was read by several unintended addressees.

senses.definition: The person who writes on or gives an address, especially in communication or linguistics.
examples: In the diagram, the addresser sends a message to the addressee.
The addresser’s intention may differ from how the listener understands it.

senses.definition: Plural of addresser: people who send messages or speak to others.
examples: Different addressers may use different levels of formality.
The study looked at how addressers adapt their language to the situation.

senses.definition: Plural of address: places where people live or organizations are located.
examples: I keep all my friends’ addresses in my phone.
The company has offices at three different addresses.

senses.definition: Third person singular of address: speaks to someone or deals with something.
examples: She addresses the class every Monday morning.
This book addresses several common questions about nutrition.

senses.definition: Full of address in the sense of skill, readiness, or resourcefulness; able to deal cleverly with situations.
examples: With an addressful manner, she handled the difficult questions.
He was praised as an addressful negotiator in the complex talks.

senses.definition: Present participle of address: speaking to someone or dealing with a problem or issue.
examples: The manager is addressing the staff about the new policy.
We are addressing the main concerns raised by customers.

senses.definition: The act of putting names and locations on letters or packages.
examples: Clear addressing of parcels reduces delivery errors.
Handwritten addressing can be hard for machines to read.

senses.definition: A machine used, especially in the past, to print names and addresses from metal plates onto envelopes or labels.
examples: The office once used an addressograph to prepare mass mailings.
Old addressograph plates were stored in heavy drawers.

senses.definition: The person who addresses something, especially the sender of a letter or message.
examples: The addressor’s details appeared on the back of the envelope.
In this model, the addressor encodes a message for the addressee.

senses.definition: Plural of addressor: people who send messages or address others.
examples: Different addressors may show different styles of politeness.
The study compared how addressors adapt to various audiences.

senses.definition: An old or poetic spelling of addressed.
examples: He addrest the king with humble words.
The letter was addrest to her across the sea.

senses.definition: Third person singular of add: puts something together with something else.
examples: She always adds sugar to her coffee.
The teacher adds new words to the list every week.
